Gate: coherence threshold for graph-context experiments

The graph input/output training approach (the separate graph-context pipeline) should be tested only once the flat model reaches a measurable level of coherence, gauged primarily by the in-context binding exact-match score, since below that level graph-versus-flat comparisons measure noise.

The graph-context pipeline (graph_transform through graph_evaluate) compares graph-structured context against flat text at matched token budgets. That comparison is only meaningful when the baseline model can actually use context: a model that cannot bind and retrieve a fact stated three sentences earlier will fail both conditions for reasons unrelated to context structure. The gate is therefore a capability threshold on the flat model, not a schedule.

Gauge and rough threshold: the in-context binding probe (node 33) is the primary instrument because it isolates exactly the capability the graph approach is meant to leverage, holding information in context, with exact-match scoring and no judge noise; the judged coherence axis is secondary corroboration. At the first full-rung measurement the binding-relevant behavior was effectively absent (semantic drift, unstable referents), so the gate is closed. A sensible opening criterion is binding exact-match rising well clear of the guessing floor (comparison questions have a two-way floor near half when the model echoes either name; retrieval questions have a floor near zero), sustained across rungs rather than at a single scale. Path to the gate: the scale-up levers in node 43 (mini on the existing corpus, then larger rungs), possibly plus mixing worldgen documents into the corpus to directly train binding. When the gate opens, the graph experiments start from the existing graph pipeline unchanged; this node only records when to start, not what to change.
